message.content:
Adversaries may leverage the "Event Triggered Execution: Trap" technique to establish persistence by executing malicious content in response to interrupt signals. This technique involves using the trap command, which allows programs and shells to specify commands that will be executed upon receiving specific signals, such as keyboard interrupts (e.g., ctrl+c, ctrl+d). By registering malicious code to be executed when these interrupts occur, adversaries can maintain a foothold on the system. The impact of this technique includes unauthorized execution of malicious scripts, potentially leading to data exfiltration, system compromise, or further propagation of malware.

Common exploitation methods include embedding trap commands in scripts or shell configurations that are likely to be executed by users or system processes. Adversaries may also modify existing scripts to include trap commands, ensuring their code runs when specific signals are received.

Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and auditing the use of trap commands within scripts and shell configurations, employing strict access controls to prevent unauthorized modifications, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and alert on suspicious use of trap commands. Regularly reviewing and validating scripts and configurations can also help identify and remove malicious trap commands.
